title: Cornish splits
subtitle: Soft white bread rolls filled with jam and clotted cream
excerpt: Cornish splits are soft, lightly yeasted white bread rolls, slightly sweeter than a dinner roll, split and filled with jam and clotted cream. Unlike scones, the dough is enriched with milk and butter and given time to rise, giving a pillowy, close-grained crumb. They are eaten the day they are made, slightly warm.
sourceNotes: Cornish splits are a traditional Cornish bread roll, served split with clotted cream and jam as part of a cream tea, distinct from the scone-based cream tea of Devon. The Cornish split is documented in Cornish cookery and local baking texts from the nineteenth century. The enriched, slightly sweet dough is the defining feature. This recipe is synthesised from the established Cornish baking tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unfilled after baking. Thaw at room temperature and warm briefly before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. The dough can be made the night before and left to rise slowly in the fridge overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan or conventional; high heat gives a soft pale crust rather than a hard shell

glossaryTerms.definition: Cornish split dough is softer and stickier than a standard bread dough because of the milk and butter. It should feel smooth and slightly tacky but not sticky enough to cling to your hands after kneading. If it tears when you stretch a small piece thinly, knead for another 2 minutes. After the first rise the dough will feel noticeably softer and more pillowy. Shape each roll by tucking the dough under itself to create a smooth top rather than pinching it into a ball.

content.text: is enriched with warm milk and butter, which means it rises more slowly than a lean white bread dough. Give it the full hour for the first rise before shaping. The rolls are baked at a high temperature but for a short time so the crust stays soft.

Combine the 450 g flour, 7 g yeast, 30 g sugar and 8 g salt in a large bowl. Add the 250 ml warm milk and 40 g softened butter. Mix to a soft dough and knead for 8 minutes until smooth and slightly tacky. Cover the bowl with a damp cloth and leave at room temperature for 1 hour until the dough has risen and feels soft and pillowy.

Knock back the dough and divide into 12 equal pieces, about 65 g each. Shape each into a smooth round roll by tucking the dough under itself. Place on a lined baking tray with about 3 cm between rolls. Cover loosely and prove for 30 minutes until noticeably puffed. Heat the oven to 200 degrees C. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until the rolls are risen and the tops are pale golden. The bases should sound hollow when tapped. Cool on a rack for 10 minutes, then split and fill with jam and clotted cream.

items.problem: Rolls have a tough, heavy crumb
items.solution: The dough was not kneaded enough, or the milk was too hot and killed the yeast. Milk should feel warm on the wrist but not hot. Knead until the dough passes the windowpane test: a small piece should stretch to near-translucency without tearing.

items.problem: Rolls spread flat rather than rising tall
items.solution: The dough was too wet or the rolls were not shaped tightly. Shape each roll by tucking the sides firmly under the base, creating surface tension on top.
